transcript: Of course, time's ahead of the Senate vote on the budget resolution that will determine whether or not Republicans can pass the tax package without Democratic support. Republicans hold a 52 to 48 Senate majority and can lose no more than two seats to pass that budget. Lee Carter, let's talk about this. The administration has been messaging this tax plan. Is it enough to get the bipartisan support? You know what I find really, really interesting is I've been testing to see how people are responding to the message that's out there. Voters really are responding well. Even Dems are responding to the message that's out there. Voters really are responding well. Even Dems are responding to the idea of simplification in a very, very meaningful way. Now, what we're seeing inside Washington is a lot of politics. People are wondering, do they have the votes? Don't they have the votes? We're hearing now, though, that Rand Paul is on board, that Corker's on board. So it looks like we could get something done, and I think that's really encouraging. I think we're seeing that play out in the markets and hopefully they're going to meet their deadline. Yeah, I mean, this week, obviously, is the big vote from the Senate. They were on vacation last week. Once that opens the door to reconciliation, Amy, you would expect that this is going to move quickly. Well, you would expect to. And I talked to some sources on Capitol Hill about how the Senate is going to be tackling the budget. And remember, the budget is the framework to be able to then move forward on tax reform. And they're saying, look, the Senate has to answer three questions. The first one being focusing on the middle class when it comes to tax reform. As you say, it looks like they're getting they get Senator Rand Paul on board with that. Secondly, they need to satisfy the deficit hawks being led by Senator Bob Corker. And thirdly, there's some other issues that are still at play which might surprise some of your viewers. We're looking at defense spending. We need John McCain to be on board with that. Even ANWR drilling in Alaska, Susan Collins in the budget. She wants to see that that is dealt with. And then you still have Ted Cruz who wants to see Obamacare repeal and replace. So they're going to be grappling with those things this week to get all those Republicans on board. And if they do, then they actually don't need to reach out to the other side. So they're budgeting one and a half trillion dollars in this tax plan, in this budget this week. Yeah, they may not have to reach out, but they're counting on, like you said, they're counting on Bob Corker and they're counting on Rand Paul. They only have that two votes, you know, very they only have two votes that they can lose on this. Yeah. Right. In terms of the Dems, they're counting on Heidi Heitkamp and they're also counting on on Donnelly. So we'll see what happens from West. Well, that goes back to the point. I mean, this tax, whether reform or cuts, is a bipartisan issue, especially when you see each party come out with their plans. And the reason being is it's so critical to the 2018 elections. If the Republicans don't get something done on the tax side, they're going to have a dismal showing next year. And they have egg on their face because they're 0 for 3 on major legislative agenda. And they ran on that. You know, the repeal and replace was a big pivotal part. And they started with that. And so if they Thank you for watching. face because they're 0 for 3 on major legislative agenda. And they ran on that. You know, the repeal and replace was a big pivotal part. They started with that. And so if they can't even get simple tax cuts done, then they're in for some deep trouble. I think they've got the votes. I think this is going to start picking up speed. I think so, too. And I think that the Dems have to be careful going into 18 because their biggest talking point is that this is a tax for, you know, tax cut for the wealthy, which simply is not true. And the American people are saying we get that. We want money back. We want more money in our paychecks. We want money for ourselves. And if the Democrats are going to keep playing out that talking point, it's not going to work. That's exactly right. The worst thing that could happen for Democrats is that Republicans pass this. It's successful. We see the economy picking up. We see more money in people's pockets. And Democrats just, you know, playing the role of obstructionists, of spoilers. That doesn't help them in the midterms. And when you look at the American people, a majority want to see tax reform. They think that this is going to move the needle on economic growth.
